region_of_origin: West Africa (origin); Malaysia and Indonesia (dominant production)
food_subgroup: Refined vegetable oil — palm oil (food industry grade)

serving.natural_language_portion: One tablespoon (14g) of refined palm oil, the standard reference serving used in Singapore hawker food service and home cooking contexts.

nutrient_source.title: Oil, palm — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171413)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171413 'Oil, palm' reports 884 kcal/100g, 100g total fat/100g, 49.3g saturated fat/100g, 37.0g monounsaturated fat/100g, 9.3g polyunsaturated fat/100g, 0g protein, 0g carbohydrate, and 15.9mg vitamin E/100g. Values match input record exactly. This is an approved primary source (USDA Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) per Project Veritas source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — Refined Palm Oil contains negligible carbohydrates (0g per 100g)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database — not applicable for zero-carbohydrate foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Refined palm oil is a pure fat (100g fat per 100g) with zero carbohydrate content. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign GI values to foods with negligible carbohydrate content as GI is undefined for such foods. Glycemic index is set to null (not_applicable) per Project Veritas q4 rule for zero-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per serving = 0.0 by definition (GL = GI/100 × available carbohydrate per serving; carbohydrate per serving = 0g).

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat (49.3g/100g, primarily palmitic acid C16:0) — LDL-raising effect may worsen cardiovascular risk associated with hypertension
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit palm oil consumption to occasional use; per tablespoon (14g) delivers 6.9g saturated fat (~35% of the 20g daily limit), and regular deep-fried hawker food consumption can substantially exceed this threshold without patient awareness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High saturated fat (49.3g/100g) associated with impaired insulin sensitivity; zero carbohydrate content means no acute glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Refined palm oil has zero carbohydrate and does not raise blood glucose acutely, but its high palmitic acid content is associated with long-term insulin resistance; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should minimise regular consumption of palm oil-fried foods and prefer canola or olive oil alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Palmitic acid (C16:0) at 49.3g saturated fat per 100g is a well-documented LDL-C raising saturated fatty acid with adverse cardiovascular risk profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit palm oil; its saturated fat content (49.3g/100g) is among the highest of commonly used cooking oils, and regular consumption via deep-fried hawker foods contributes substantially to LDL-C elevation and cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Palmitic acid activates TLR4 pro-inflammatory signalling; partially offset by tocotrienol vitamin E (15.9mg/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit palm oil-fried foods as excess palmitic acid may amplify systemic inflammation, though the tocotrienol fraction of vitamin E provides partial antioxidant offset at moderate dietary amounts.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Refined palm oil is the dominant commercial cooking fat used in Singapore's hawker food service industry, extracted from the fruit of oil palm trees (Elaeis guineensis) native to West Africa but predominantly cultivated in Malaysia and Indonesia, which together produce approximately 85% of global supply. It is a pure fat (100g fat per 100g) with zero protein, zero carbohydrate, and zero dietary fibre. Its energy density is 884 kcal per 100g, making it one of the most calorie-dense foods in the diet. Per tablespoon (14g): approximately 124 kcal and 6.9g saturated fat. The fat profile is approximately 49.3g saturated fat (primarily palmitic acid C16:0), 37g monounsaturated fat (primarily oleic acid), and 9.3g polyunsaturated fat per 100g. Refined palm oil has a high smoke point (~235°C), long shelf life, and semi-solid texture at room temperature, making it ideal for commercial deep-frying of roti prata, fried chicken, fritters, and most hawker fried dishes. It is rarely disclosed on menus, meaning patients who consume deep-fried hawker foods regularly are exposed to substantial palm oil-derived saturated fat without awareness. Nutritionally, its high palmitic acid content is associated with LDL-C elevation and long-term insulin resistance risk. It contains 15.9mg vitamin E per 100g, predominantly as tocotrienols, which have antioxidant properties but do not offset the cardiovascular and metabolic concerns of regular high-volume consumption. HPB Singapore recommends replacing palm oil with canola or olive oil in home cooking. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g per 100g); GI is undefined for pure fats per University of Sydney GI Database methodology.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171413 for refined palm oil, an approved primary source with exact match. Values are consistent with well-established food composition data for this commodity oil. Input record confidence was 1.0 with verified provenance.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero as refined palm oil is a pure fat with no protein fraction — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171413. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as refined palm oil contains no carbohydrate — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171413. dietary_fibre_g, sugar_g, sodium_mg, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, all B vitamins, vitamin_d_mcg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171413 for refined palm oil — this is a pure refined fat with no mineral, vitamin (except vitamin E tocotrienols), or water content. vitamin_e_mg is 15.9mg/100g (tocotrienol-rich fraction) per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171413. monounsaturated_fat_g (37.0g/100g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (9.3g/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171413 fatty acid profile for palm oil. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g carbohydrate per 100g); glycemic_index set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable per Project Veritas q4 rule for zero-carbohydrate foods; GI is undefined for pure fats per University of Sydney GI Database methodology. FoodEx2 code A00LK assigned based on EFSA FoodEx2 classification for palm oil — defensible exact match. SNOMED CT code not assigned — no specific clinical terminology code identified for refined palm oil as a food ingredient. The input record used 棕榈油 with language tag 'en' which is incorrect; corrected to 'chinese' in aliases_by_language. chinese_traditional form 棕櫚油 added based on standard Traditional Chinese rendering. Tamil and Hindi local names not populated as this is a global commodity cooking fat, not a Singaporean dish; the G6 multilingual rule applies to Singaporean dishes specifically — this record is classified as Cooking Fat / Food Industry and does not trigger the Singaporean dish multilingual requirement.

Iteration 3: glycemic_index confirmed as null (not 0) — this is correct per q4 rule; previous validator noted 'glycemic_index must be numeric' but the Project Veritas q4 critical rule explicitly states null is required for zero-carbohydrate foods and that setting glycemic_index=0 will cause a q4 gate failure. null is the correct value.
